```{r}
                aes(x = x , xend=xend, y = y , yend = yend ) ,
                arrow = arrow(length = unit(3, "pt")) ,
                color=line_color_2 , linewidth=1 ) +
  geom_point( data=data.frame( data_prop=z_ex/n_ex , y=rep(data_ht,5) ),
              aes( x=data_prop , y=y ) , shape="diamond" ,
              color=point_color_1 , size=6  ) +
  annotate( "text" , x=0.2 , y=data_ht , label=deparse(bquote(z[j]/n[j])), parse=TRUE , size=5, hjust="right") +
  geom_point( data=data.frame( theta=param_opt[1:5] , y=rep(theta_ht,5) ),
              aes( x=theta , y=y ) , shape="circle" ,
              color=point_color_1 , size=5  ) +
  annotate( "text" , x=0.2 , y=theta_ht , 
            label=deparse(bquote(theta[j])), parse=TRUE , size=5, hjust="right") +
  annotate( "text" , x=0.5 , y=2.0 , size=5 ,
            label=deparse( 
              bquote( omega *" = "* .(unname(param_opt["omega"])) 
                      *", "* kappa *" = "* .(round(unname(param_opt["kappa"]),2)) )
            ),
            parse=TRUE ) +
  # labs( title = "MLE, Shrunken Parameters" ,
  #       subtitle = bquote(
  #                    "Likelihood = "*.(signif(exp(-nll_opt),5)) 
  #                    # *
  #                    # ", " 
  #                    # * 
  #                    # "NLL = "*.(signif(nll_opt,5))
  #                  )) +
  labs( title = "MLE, Shrunken Parameters" ) +
  annotate( "text", x=0.5, y = Inf, hjust = 0.5, vjust = 1.5,
            label = deparse(bquote("Likelihood = "*.(signif(exp(-nll_opt),5)))),
            parse = TRUE ) +
  theme( plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0.5) ,
         axis.ticks.y=element_blank() , 
         axis.text.y=element_blank() , 
         axis.title.y=element_blank() ) + 
  xlab( bquote(theta) )

( g_try | g_opt )
```

```{r}
#| echo: FALSE
#| warning: FALSE
#| message: FALSE
#| label: fig-shrinkage
#| fig-width: 6.0
#| fig-height: 3.0
#| fig-cap: "Diamonds mark the values of $z_j/n_j$ on the horizontal axis; dots mark the values of $\\theta_j$. Distributions show beta densities with the annotated $\\omega$ and $\\kappa$. Left panel shows a candidate choice of parameters without shrinkage. Right panel shows the maximum-likelihood estimate (MLE) of parameters, in which $\\theta_j$ show shrinkage toward $\\omega$."
#| fig-pos: "!tb"

# Densities
fig_df <- bind_rows(param_try, param_opt) |>
  mutate(model = c("try", "opt"),
         a = omega * (kappa - 2) + 1,
         b = (1 - omega) * (kappa - 2) + 1) |>
  select(model, omega, kappa, a, b) |>
  expand_grid(theta = seq(from = 0, to = 1, length.out = 501)) |>
  mutate(density = dbeta(x = theta, shape1 = a, shape2 = b)) |> 
  mutate(model = factor(model, 
                        levels = c("try", "opt"),
                        labels = c("Not shrunken parameters",
                                   "MLE, shrunken parameters")))

# Point estimates
estimate_df <- fig_df |> 
  distinct(model) |> 
  expand_grid(ratio = z_ex / n_ex) |> 
  mutate(j = rep(1:5, times = 2),
         theta = c(param_try[1:5], param_opt[1:5])) |> 
  pivot_longer(cols = c(ratio, theta), values_to = "theta") |> 
  mutate(density = ifelse(name == "ratio", -1.7, -0.7))

# Likelihood values
text_1_df <- fig_df |> 
  distinct(model) |> 
  mutate(lik = ifelse(model == "Not shrunken parameters", nll_try, nll_opt)) |> 
  mutate(label = str_c("Likelihood==", signif(exp(-lik), 5)),
         # label = str_c("{NLL==", signif(lik, 5), "}*', '*Likelihood==", signif(exp(-lik), 5)),
         theta = 0.5,  # 0
         density = 4.8)

# Omega and kappa values
text_2_df <- fig_df |> 
  distinct(model, omega, kappa) |> 
  mutate(label = str_c("{omega==", omega, "}*', '*kappa==", round(kappa, digits = 2)))

# Estimate types
text_3_df <- estimate_df |> 
  distinct(model, name, density) |> 
  mutate(label = ifelse(name == "ratio",
                        "italic(z[j])/italic(n[j])",
                        "theta[italic(j)]"),
         theta = 0.25)

# Plot
fig_df |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = theta, y = density)) +
  geom_area(fill = fill_color_1) +  # Densities
  geom_text(data = text_1_df,       # Likelihood values
            aes(label = label),
            parse = TRUE, size = 3.75, vjust = 0) +
  geom_text(data = text_2_df,       # Omega and kappa values
            aes(x = 0.5, y = 2, label = label),
            parse = TRUE, size = 4.5) +
  geom_text(data = text_3_df,       # Estimate types
            aes(label = label),
            hjust = 1, parse = TRUE, size = 3.75) +
  geom_line(data = estimate_df,     # Point estimate lines
            aes(group = j)) +
  geom_point(data = estimate_df,    # Point estimate shapes
             aes(color = name, shape = name, size = name)) +
  scale_x_continuous(expression(theta), breaks = 0:4 / 4) +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL, breaks = NULL, limits = c(-2, 5)) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(point_color_2, point_color_1), breaks = NULL) +
  scale_shape_manual(values = c(18, 19), breaks = NULL) +
  scale_size_manual(values = c(4, 3), breaks = NULL) +
  facet_wrap(~ model)
```

@fig-shrinkage illustrates two sets of parameters and their resulting likelihoods, for a simple example in which there are `r length(z_ex)` individuals all with $N=$ `r n_ex[1]` and proportions $z/N$ of `r z_ex[1]/n_ex[1]`, `r z_ex[2]/n_ex[2]`, `r z_ex[3]/n_ex[3]`, `r z_ex[4]/n_ex[4]`, and `r z_ex[5]/n_ex[5]`, as shown in @fig-shrinkage. The left panel of @fig-shrinkage shows candidate parameter values with $\theta_j$ matching $z_j/n_j$, and $\kappa$ set so the beta distribution is essentially flat. By contrast, the right panel of @fig-shrinkage shows the MLE values of the parameters. You can see that the $\theta_j$ values are shrunken together toward $\omega$, and the larger $\kappa$ makes the beta distribution peaked over the $\theta_j$ values. Annotations in the two panels show the likelihoods. The likelihood values (`r signif( exp(-nll_try) , 5 )` and `r signif( exp(-nll_opt) , 5)`) may seem to be very small, but this is a mathematical consequence of multiplying many `bernoulli` probabilities together. The exact magnitude of the likelihood is not usually important; what matters is the likelihood relative to other choices of parameter values. *The maximum likelihood is `r round( exp( -nll_opt - -nll_try ) , 2)` times higher than the likelihood of the not-shrunken parameter values.*

Intuitively, shrinkage occurs because the data from all individuals influence the higher-level distribution, which in turn influences the estimates of each individual. The estimate of $\theta_j$ is a compromise between the data $z_j/N_s$ of that individual and the group-level distribution informed by all the individuals. 
<!-- Mathematically, the compromise is expressed by having to jointly maximize  -->
<!-- $\prod_{i|j} \mbox{bern}( y_{i|j} | \theta_j )$  -->
<!-- and  -->
<!-- $\mbox{beta}( \theta_j \, | \, \omega (\kappa\!-\!2)\!+\!1 , (1\!-\!\omega) (\kappa\!-\!2)\!+\!1 )$. -->
<!-- The first term is maximized by $\theta_j = z_j/N_s$ and the second term is maximized by $\theta_j = \omega$.  As $\theta_j$ is pulled away from $z_j/N_s$ toward $\omega$, the first term gets smaller but the second term gets larger. The MLE finds the best compromise between the two factors. -->
Mathematically, shrinkage increases the overall likelihood because, even though $\mbox{bernoulli}(z_j,n_j|\theta_j)$ decreases when $\theta_j \ne z_j/n_j$, $\mbox{beta}(\theta_j|\omega,\kappa)$ increases when the $\theta_j$'s shrink toward $\omega$ and $\kappa$ increases.

### Shrinkage is affected by the prior {#sec-shrinkage_prior}

In the example of @fig-shrinkage, shrinkage occurred from maximum likelihood alone, without any influence of a prior distribution. However, in a Bayesian model the prior can be an additional source of shrinkage. Indeed, some priors are intentionally designed to impose more or less shrinkage. 

For example, the prior distribution in @fig-omega_kappa_prior imposes shrinkage on the $\theta_j$ values to the extent that large values of $\kappa$ are preferred by the gamma prior. Remember, the value of $\kappa$ is the concentration, or consistency, of the $\theta_j$ values generated by the gamma prior, so a large $\kappa$ implies concentrated $\theta_j$ values. When the gamma prior on $\kappa$ encourages large values of $\kappa$ and discourages small values of $\kappa$, then shrinkage can be extreme. Shrinkage is least when the gamma prior *dis*courages large values of $\kappa$. 

```{r}
#| echo: FALSE
#| message: FALSE
#| warning: FALSE

# Extract existing MEDIUM kappa prior results.
# These values are used only for reporting in text, not for running models.
theta_tt_q50_medium <- stan_fit_diag |>  
  filter( variable == "theta[1]"  | variable == "theta[28]" ) |> 
  select( "q50" )

# New contrasting case: WIDE kappa prior constants.
gamma_mode_wide <- 10
gamma_sd_wide <- 100
gsr <- gamma_s_r_from_mode_sd( mode = gamma_mode_wide , sd = gamma_sd_wide )
gamma_s <- gsr$shape
gamma_r <- gsr$rate
bab <- beta_a_b_from_mode_kappa( mode = 0.5 , kappa = 2.001 )
beta_a <- bab$a
beta_b <- bab$b
data_list_wide <- list( y = y, j = j, n_j = n_j, n_t = n_t ,
                   beta_a = beta_a , beta_b = beta_b , 
                   gamma_s = gamma_s , gamma_r = gamma_r )
if ( run_fresh ) { 
  cmdstan_fit_wide <- cmdstan_model_obj$sample(
    data = data_list_wide,
    chains = 4, iter_warmup = 200, iter_sampling = 5200, seed = 47 )
  cmdstan_fit_wide$save_object( file = paste0(save_file_folder,"/_cmdstan_fit_wide.RDS") )
} else {
  cmdstan_fit_wide <- readRDS( paste0(save_file_folder,"/_cmdstan_fit_wide.RDS") )
}
draws_df_wide <- as_draws_df( cmdstan_fit_wide ) 
diag_tbl_wide <- summarize_draws_dbda3( draws_df_wide ) |>
  filter( variable != "lp__" & variable != "kappa_minus_two" )
theta_tt_q50_wide <- diag_tbl_wide |> 
  filter( variable == "theta[1]"  | variable == "theta[28]" ) |> 
  select( "q50" )

# Specify NARROW kappa prior constants:
gamma_mode_narrow <- 0.5
gamma_sd_narrow <- 0.5
gsr <- gamma_s_r_from_mode_sd( mode=gamma_mode_narrow , sd=gamma_sd_narrow )
gamma_s <- gsr$shape
gamma_r <- gsr$rate
bab <- beta_a_b_from_mode_kappa( mode=0.5 , kappa=2.001 )
beta_a <- bab$a
beta_b <- bab$b
data_list_narrow <- list( y = y, j = j, n_j = n_j, n_t = n_t ,
                   beta_a = beta_a , beta_b = beta_b , 
                   gamma_s = gamma_s , gamma_r = gamma_r )
if ( run_fresh ) { 
  cmdstan_fit_narrow <- cmdstan_model_obj$sample(
    data = data_list_narrow,
    chains = 4, iter_warmup = 200, iter_sampling = 5200, seed = 47 )
  cmdstan_fit_narrow$save_object( file = paste0(save_file_folder,"/_cmdstan_fit_narrow.RDS") )
} else {
  cmdstan_fit_narrow <- readRDS( paste0(save_file_folder,"/_cmdstan_fit_narrow.RDS") )
}
draws_df_narrow <- as_draws_df( cmdstan_fit_narrow ) 
diag_tbl_narrow <- summarize_draws_dbda3( draws_df_narrow ) |>
  filter( variable != "lp__" & variable != "kappa_minus_two" )
theta_tt_q50_narrow <- diag_tbl_narrow |> 
  filter( variable == "theta[1]"  | variable == "theta[28]" ) |> 
  select( "q50" )
```

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| warning: FALSE
#| message: FALSE
#| label: fig-shrinkage_kappa
#| fig-cap: "When analyzing the therapeutic-touch data, shrinkage on individual estimates depends on the prior on $\\kappa - 2$. When the prior emphasizes large values of $\\kappa - 2$, there is greater shrinkage than when the prior suppresses large values of $\\kappa - 2$."
#| fig-width: 5.0
#| fig-height: 2.0
#| fig-pos: "!tb"

cmdstan_fit_draws <- as_draws_df(cmdstan_fit)
cmdstan_fit_wide_draws <- as_draws_df(cmdstan_fit_wide)
cmdstan_fit_narrow_draws <- as_draws_df(cmdstan_fit_narrow)

bind_rows(
  # cmdstan_fit_draws,
  cmdstan_fit_wide_draws,
  cmdstan_fit_narrow_draws ) |> 
  mutate( prior = c( # rep("Medium prior, \nhigh and low values", 
    #      nrow(cmdstan_fit_draws)),
    rep("Large values\nemphasized", 
        nrow(cmdstan_fit_wide_draws)),
    rep("Large values\nsuppressed", 
        nrow(cmdstan_fit_narrow_draws)) ) # |> 
    # factor(levels = c("narrow", "medium", "wide")) 
  ) |> 
  # mutate(`theta[28]-theta[1]` = `theta[28]` - `theta[1]`) |>
  pivot_longer(cols = c(`theta[1]`, `theta[28]`) ) |> # , `theta[28]-theta[1]`)) |>

  ggplot(aes(x = value, y = prior)) +
  stat_histinterval(point_interval = median_qi, .width = 0.95,
                    breaks = 40, color = line_color_1, 
                    fill = fill_color_1, normalize = "panels") +
  scale_x_continuous(NULL, breaks = -2:2 / 2) +
  scale_y_discrete(expression("Prior on "*(kappa-2)), 
                   expand = expansion(mult = 0.05)) +
  expand_limits(x = 0:1) +
  # ggtitle( expression("Posterior shrinkage affected by the prior on "*(kappa - 2)) ) +
  facet_wrap(~ name, labeller = label_parsed, scales = "free_x", strip.position = "bottom")  +
  theme(axis.text.y = element_text(vjust = 0.0),
        strip.background = element_blank(),
        strip.placement = "outside",
        strip.text = element_text(size = base_size, margin = margin(t = 0, b = 0)))
```

You can see for yourself by re-running the therapeutic-touch example with different specifications of the gamma prior. This is easy to do because the Stan model was specified with the prior constants delivered with the data instead of being fixed in the model specification itself. 

Recall from @tbl-diag_tbl_median_09 that when the gamma prior on $\kappa-2$ has mode=`r gamma_mode` and sd=`r gamma_sd`, then the posterior median for $\theta_1$ is `r round(theta_tt_q50_medium[1,1],3)` and `r round(theta_tt_q50_medium[2,1],3)` for $\theta_{28}$, which yields a difference of $\theta_{28} - \theta_{1} =$ `r  round(theta_tt_q50_medium[2,1] - theta_tt_q50_medium[1,1],3)`, as was illustrated in @fig-omega_kappa.

If instead the gamma prior on $\kappa-2$ is set to favor larger values, with mode=`r gamma_mode_wide` and sd=`r gamma_sd_wide`, then the posterior median for $\theta_1$ is `r round(theta_tt_q50_wide[1,1],3)` and `r round(theta_tt_q50_wide[2,1],3)` for $\theta_{28}$, which yields a difference $\theta_{28} - \theta_{1}$ of only `r  round(theta_tt_q50_wide[2,1] - theta_tt_q50_wide[1,1],3)`. In other words, they are shrunken together more. See @fig-shrinkage_kappa.
By contrast, when the gamma prior on $\kappa-2$ is set to suppress larger values, with mode=`r gamma_mode_narrow` and sd=`r gamma_sd_narrow`, then the posterior median for $\theta_1$ is `r round(theta_tt_q50_narrow[1,1],3)` and `r round(theta_tt_q50_narrow[2,1],3)` for $\theta_{28}$, which yields a difference $\theta_{28} - \theta_{1}$ of `r  round(theta_tt_q50_narrow[2,1] - theta_tt_q50_narrow[1,1],3)`. Thus, they are shrunken together less. Again, see @fig-shrinkage_kappa.
<!-- The exercises at the end of this chapter for a bit more detail about generating these results. -->

Hierarchical models typically have high-level parameters (such as $\kappa$ in this example) that control the concentration, or spread, of lower-level parameters (such as $\theta_j$ in this example). The priors on those higher-level concentration or spread parameters will affect the degree of shrinkage.

In summary, we are emphasizing that shrinkage occurs in hierarchical models from maximizing likelihood without the influence of a prior, but priors can also influence shrinkage. Often, priors encourage more shrinkage, but sometimes priors can inhibit shrinkage.


### Shrinkage is affected by amount of data {#sec-shrinkage_data}

```{r}
#| echo: FALSE
#| message: FALSE
#| warning: FALSE

# re-set original example prior:
gsr <- gamma_s_r_from_mode_sd( mode = gamma_mode , sd = gamma_sd )
gamma_s <- gsr$shape
gamma_r <- gsr$rate

# Create Data Set A:
n_ps <- 10 # number flips per subject
n_jpp <- 10 # number subjects per proportion
y <- vector()
p_vec <- c(3,7)
for ( z_ps in p_vec ) {
  y <- c( y , rep( c(rep(1,z_ps),rep(0,n_ps-z_ps)) , n_jpp ) )
}
j <- rep( 1:(length(p_vec)*n_jpp) , each=n_ps )
n_j <- length( unique( j ) )          # number of subjects
n_t <- length( y )                    # total number trials
data_list_A <- list( y = y, j = j, n_j = n_j, n_t = n_t ,
                   beta_a = beta_a , beta_b = beta_b , 
                   gamma_s = gamma_s , gamma_r = gamma_r )
# Generate MCMC: 
if ( run_fresh ) { 
  cmdstan_fit_A <- cmdstan_model_obj$sample(
    data = data_list_A,
    chains = 4, iter_warmup = 200, iter_sampling = 5200, seed = 47 )
  cmdstan_fit_A$save_object( file = paste0(save_file_folder,"/_cmdstan_fit_A.RDS") )
} else {
  cmdstan_fit_A <- readRDS( paste0(save_file_folder,"/_cmdstan_fit_A.RDS") )
}
# Collect posterior info:
draws_df_A <- as_draws_df( cmdstan_fit_A ) 
diag_tbl_A <- summarize_draws_dbda3( draws_df_A ) |>
  filter( variable != "lp__" & variable != "kappa_minus_two" )
post_A <- diag_tbl_A |> 
  filter( variable %in% c("theta[1]","theta[20]","omega","kappa") ) |> 
  select( c("q2.5","q50","q97.5") )
A_t1 <- post_A[1,"q50"]
A_t20 <- post_A[2,"q50"]
A_omega_eti <- post_A[3,c("q2.5","q97.5")]
A_kappa <- post_A[4,"q50"]

# Create Data Set B:
n_ps <- 100 # number flips per subject
n_jpp <- 1 # number subjects per proportion
y <- vector()
# p_vec <- c(3,4,5,6,7)
p_vec <- c(3,7)
for ( z_ps in p_vec*10 ) {
  y <- c( y , rep( c(rep(1,z_ps),rep(0,n_ps-z_ps)) , n_jpp ) )
}
j <- rep( 1:(length(p_vec)*n_jpp) , each=n_ps )
n_j <- length( unique( j ) )          # number of subjects
n_t <- length( y )                    # total number trials
data_list_B <- list( y = y, j = j, n_j = n_j, n_t = n_t ,
                   beta_a = beta_a , beta_b = beta_b , 
                   gamma_s = gamma_s , gamma_r = gamma_r )
# Generate MCMC:
if ( run_fresh ) { 
  cmdstan_fit_B <- cmdstan_model_obj$sample(
    data = data_list_B,
    chains = 4, iter_warmup = 200, iter_sampling = 5200, seed = 47 )
  cmdstan_fit_B$save_object( file = paste0(save_file_folder,"/_cmdstan_fit_B.RDS") )
} else {
  cmdstan_fit_B <- readRDS( paste0(save_file_folder,"/_cmdstan_fit_B.RDS") )
}
# Collect posterior info:
draws_df_B <- as_draws_df( cmdstan_fit_B ) 
diag_tbl_B <- summarize_draws_dbda3( draws_df_B ) |>
  filter( variable != "lp__" & variable != "kappa_minus_two" )
post_B <- diag_tbl_B |> 
  filter( variable %in% c("theta[1]","theta[2]","omega","kappa") ) |> 
  select( c("q2.5","q50","q97.5") )
B_t1 <- post_B[1,"q50"]
B_t2 <- post_B[2,"q50"]
B_omega_eti <- post_B[3,c("q2.5","q97.5")]
B_kappa <- post_B[4,"q50"]


# Create Data Set C:
# mix of large n, small n subjects
y <- vector() 
j <- vector()
#
y <- c( y , rep( c(rep(1,3),rep(0,10-3)) , 5 ) )
j <- c( j , rep( 1:5 , each=10 ) )
y <- c( y , rep( c(rep(1,15),rep(0,50-15)) , 1 ) )
j <- c( j , rep( 6 , each=50 ) )
#
# y <- c( y , rep( c(rep(1,4),rep(0,10-4)) , 5 ) )
# j <- c( j , rep( max(j)+1:5 , each=10 ) )
# y <- c( y , rep( c(rep(1,20),rep(0,50-20)) , 1 ) )
# j <- c( j , rep( max(j)+1 , each=50 ) )
# #
# y <- c( y , rep( c(rep(1,5),rep(0,10-5)) , 5 ) )
# j <- c( j , rep( max(j)+1:5 , each=10 ) )
# y <- c( y , rep( c(rep(1,25),rep(0,50-25)) , 1 ) )
# j <- c( j , rep( max(j)+1 , each=50 ) )
# #
# y <- c( y , rep( c(rep(1,6),rep(0,10-6)) , 5 ) )
# j <- c( j , rep( max(j)+1:5 , each=10 ) )
# y <- c( y , rep( c(rep(1,30),rep(0,50-30)) , 1 ) )
# j <- c( j , rep( max(j)+1 , each=50 ) )
#
y <- c( y , rep( c(rep(1,7),rep(0,10-7)) , 5 ) )
j <- c( j , rep( max(j)+1:5 , each=10 ) )
y <- c( y , rep( c(rep(1,35),rep(0,50-35)) , 1 ) )
j <- c( j , rep( max(j)+1 , each=50 ) )
#
n_j <- length( unique( j ) )          # number of subjects
n_t <- length( y )                    # total number trials

data_list_C <- list( y = y, j = j, n_j = n_j, n_t = n_t ,
                   beta_a = beta_a , beta_b = beta_b , 
                   gamma_s = gamma_s , gamma_r = gamma_r )
# Generate MCMC:
if ( run_fresh ) { 
  cmdstan_fit_C <- cmdstan_model_obj$sample(
    data = data_list_C,
    chains = 4, iter_warmup = 200, iter_sampling = 5200, seed = 47 )
  cmdstan_fit_C$save_object( file = paste0(save_file_folder,"/_cmdstan_fit_C.RDS") )
} else {
  cmdstan_fit_C <- readRDS( paste0(save_file_folder,"/_cmdstan_fit_C.RDS") )
}
# Collect posterior info:
draws_df_C <- as_draws_df( cmdstan_fit_C ) 
diag_tbl_C <- summarize_draws_dbda3( draws_df_C ) |>
  filter( variable != "lp__" & variable != "kappa_minus_two" )
post_C <- diag_tbl_C |> 
  filter( variable %in% c("theta[1]","theta[6]","theta[7]","theta[12]","omega","kappa") ) |> 
  select( c("q2.5","q50","q97.5") )
C_t1 <- post_C[1,"q50"]
C_t20 <- post_C[2,"q50"]
C_omega_eti <- post_C[3,c("q2.5","q97.5")]
C_kappa <- post_C[4,"q50"]
```


Consider the following two different data sets:

\noindent
*Data Set A:*

  * 10 subjects with $z=3, n=10$
  * 10 subjects with $z=7, n=10$

\noindent
*Data Set B:*

  * 1 subject with $z=30, n=100$
  * 1 subject with $z=70, n=100$

\noindent
In both data sets, the total number of flips is 200. In both data sets, half the subjects have 30% heads, and half the subjects have 70% heads.
If we model these two data sets using the hierarchical model in @fig-dependstructjcoins, will the two data sets yield identical parameter estimates? The answer is *no*. 


```{r}
#| echo: false
#| warning: FALSE
#| message: FALSE
#| label: fig-shrinkage_depends_on_data
#| fig-cap: "Shrinkage depends on amount of data. Data sets A, B, and C are shown in the top, middle, and bottom panels. Diamonds show $z_j/n_j$ for each individual $j$, with diamond size corresponding to $n_j$. The posterior estimates of the parameters are shown by dots at their median and line segments for the 95% ETI. Notice that shrinkage of $\\theta_j$ is stronger for smaller $n_j$, and the estimate of $\\omega$ is more certain when there are more individuals."
#| fig-width: 6.5
#| fig-height: 5.5
#| fig-pos: "!tb"


# collect posterior info for plotting:
diagA <- diag_tbl_A |> select("variable" | starts_with("q")) |> mutate(set = "A")
diagB <- diag_tbl_B |> select("variable" | starts_with("q")) |> mutate(set = "B")
diagC <- diag_tbl_C |> select("variable" | starts_with("q")) |> mutate(set = "C")
diagAll <- data.frame( rbind( diagA , diagB , diagC ) )
diagAll$variable <- factor( diagAll$variable, 
                      levels=c(paste0("theta[",1:20,"]"),"omega","kappa") )
# collect data info for plotting:
dataA <- data_list_A |> data.frame() |> group_by( j ) |> 
  summarize( p = sum(y)/length(y) , n = length(y) ) |> mutate(set = "A")
dataB <- data_list_B |> data.frame() |> group_by( j ) |> 
  summarize( p = sum(y)/length(y) , n = length(y) ) |> mutate(set = "B")
dataC <- data_list_C |> data.frame() |> group_by( j ) |> 
  summarize( p = sum(y)/length(y) , n = length(y) ) |> mutate(set = "C")
dataAll <- rbind( dataA, dataB, dataC )

ggplot( data = diagAll |> filter( substr(variable,1,5)=="theta" 
                                 | substr(variable,1,5)=="omega" ) ) +
  geom_segment( aes( x=`q2.5` , xend=`q97.5` , y=variable ) , 
                color=line_color_1 ) +
  geom_point( aes( x=q50 , y=variable ) , size=2.0 , color=point_color_1 ) +
  geom_point( data = dataAll , 
              aes( x=p , y=j , size =  n ) , 
              show.legend = FALSE ,
              color=point_color_2 , position = position_nudge(y = -0.0) ,
              shape = 18 ) +
  scale_size( n , range=c(2,5) ) +
  scale_x_continuous( expression(theta[italic(j)]~or~italic(z[j])/italic(n[j])~or~omega), 
                      # breaks = c(0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 0.9), 
                      # limits = c(0.05,0.95) 
                      breaks = c(0, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 1), limits = 0:1
                      ) +  
  scale_y_discrete("Parameter", labels = ggplot2:::parse_safe) +
  facet_grid( set ~ . , scales = "free_y" , space = "free_y" ) +
  theme(axis.text.y = element_text(hjust = 0))
```


<!-- Before running the model on the two data sets, test your intuition: -->

<!--   * In which data set will there be more shrinkage of the estimates of individual subjects?  -->
<!--   * In which data set will the 95% ETI on $\omega$ be narrower, that is, more precise? -->

Data set A will exhibit more shrinkage of individual parameter estimates toward $\omega$ because there are fewer data (flips) per individual. With less data to anchor each individual estimate, the estimates are more prone to shrinkage toward the group mode. Data set A will also show a narrower credible interval on $\omega$, because there are more subjects informing it, and with higher $\kappa$ (more shrinkage) there is also more certainty about $\omega$.
In data set B, the estimates of the individual $\theta_j$ will be closer to the data proportions because each individual has more information anchoring the estimate. On the other hand, because there are fewer subjects (and smaller $\kappa$), the estimate of $\omega$ will be less precise.

As shown in @fig-shrinkage_depends_on_data, when data set A is run through the same model as used for the therapeutic-touch example (see @fig-dependstructjcoins), the posterior median estimate of $\theta$ for subjects with $z_j/n_j = 0.3$ is `r round(A_t1,2)` and the posterior median estimate of $\theta$ for subjects with $z_j/n_j = 0.7$ is `r round(A_t20,2)`. 
The 95% ETI on $\omega$ extends from `r round(A_omega_eti[1],2)` to `r round(A_omega_eti[2],2)`. Although not shown in @fig-shrinkage_depends_on_data, the posterior median estimate of $\kappa$ is `r round(A_kappa,2)`. Of course, all these numerical values are subject to MCMC wobble.

When data set B is run through the same model, @fig-shrinkage_depends_on_data shows that the posterior median estimate of $\theta$ for subjects with $z_j/n_j = 0.3$ is `r round(B_t1,2)` and the posterior median estimate of $\theta$ for subjects with $z_j/n_j = 0.7$ is `r round(B_t2,2)`, and the 95% ETI on $\omega$ extends from `r round(B_omega_eti[1],2)` to `r round(B_omega_eti[2],2)`.
Although not shown in @fig-shrinkage_depends_on_data, the posterior median estimate of $\kappa$ is `r round(B_kappa,2)`.
 
Thus, shrinkage of $\theta_j$ is greater for the data with smaller $n_j$ per subject; that is, shrinkage of $\theta_j$ is greater for data set A than for data set B. And the uncertainty of $\omega$ (i.e., the width of its ETI) is smaller for the data with more subjects; that is, the group-level mode has more certainty for data set A than for data set B.

Now consider a similar data set with the same total number of flips, the same distribution of proportions overall, but with a mix of subjects with different amounts of data:

\noindent
*Data Set C:*

  * 5 subjects with $z=3, n=10$, and 1 subject with $z=15, n=50$
  * 5 subjects with $z=7, n=10$, and 1 subject with $z=35, n=50$

\noindent
Will all subjects with the same proportion of heads, $z_j/n_j$, have the same estimated underlying probabilities correct, $\theta_j$? The answer is *no*. *Individuals with smaller $n_j$ will be shrunken toward the group mode more than individuals with larger $n_j$* (when their data proportions are the same). @fig-shrinkage_depends_on_data shows the results: The subjects with $n=50$ show less shrinkage than the subjects with $n=10$.

This differential shrinkage is not only mathematically correct, but it also is intuitively reasonable: When we do not have much data about an individual, our best estimate of that individual's ability should be strongly influenced by what is typical of the group we know they are in. Indeed, if we have *no* data from an individual but know the group that individual is in, then our best estimate of their ability is the group's central tendency. With more individual data, our estimate of the individual can shift away from the group central tendency toward the proportion exhibited by the individual.



### Implications for analysis and design

Differential shrinkage, depending on amount of individual data, can have dramatic implications in real applications. For example, suppose the success of a medical treatment is measured at many different hospitals, with each hospital being an "individual" in a hierarchical model. Some hospitals will have many cases of the treatment, while other hospitals will have far fewer cases. The estimated success rate of a few-cases hospital will be shrunken much more than the estimated success rate of a many-cases hospital. Notice the shrinkage goes toward the mode across hospitals, so individual hospitals with low proportions of success have their estimates shrunken upward, and individual hospitals with high proportions of success have their estimates shrunken downward. 

As another example, consider baseball players, for whom the "batting average" is computed as the number of hits out of number of opportunities at bat. Some players get many opportunities at bat, while other players get relatively few. When estimating a player's batting ability, it makes sense to inform the estimate with what is typical of other players who have the same primary fielding position. So, we use the hierarchical model we have been using throughout this chapter. Consequently, players with only a few opportunities at bat will have their estimates shrunken toward what is typical of their position much more than players with many opportunities at bat. (The Second Edition of this book, @Kruschke2015book, in its Section 9.5.1, had an extended example of batting averages for different positions.)

These characteristics of shrinkage also have important implications for research design. If you have a choice of collecting a small amount of data from many subjects, or a lot of data from a few subjects, your decision should be governed by whether you want accurate estimates of individuals or an accurate estimate of the group overall. If you want accurate estimates of individuals (i.e., $\theta_j$), run more trials per individual. If you want an accurate estimate of the group-level mode (i.e., $\omega$), run more individuals. The issue of sample-size determination will be discussed more in @sec-power, along with so-called "power analysis."

When using a hierarchical (a.k.a., multilevel) model, it is assumed that all individuals really are members of the same meaningful group, and are all representative of that group. This is a semantic and usually causal assumption about the world, insofar as the group assignment indicates some underlying structure in that group that influences all the individuals similarly. 
Mathematically, the model also assumes that all individuals are "exchangably" drawn from that group, which means that the joint probability of the individual parameters is the same for any ordering of the individuals.
<!-- ^[For a discussion of exchangeability, see <https://vasishth.github.io/bayescogsci/book/ch-hierarchical.html#exchangeability-and-hierarchical-models>.]  -->
Exchangeability is a slightly weaker condition than independent and identically distributed *(iid)*, so if the individual parameters are *iid* then they are exchangeable.
All of these assumptions are in the mathematical expression that says the individual abilities are generated from a beta distribution: for all $j$, $\theta_j \sim \mbox{beta}(\omega,\kappa)$ (see the full expression in Eqn. \ref{eq:tt_theta}). The point is that this mathematical description makes strong assumptions that should reflect meaningful structure in the world.

If the assumed grouping is not usefully reflective of real patterns in the data, then the shrinkage induced by the grouping could be spurious and misleading. As just one example, consider again the estimation of a baseball player's batting average. If the player is grouped with pitchers, their estimated batting average will be shrunken toward what is typical of pitchers, and if the player is grouped with catchers, their estimated batting average will be shrunken toward what is typical of catchers. It turns out that catchers in general have much higher batting averages than pitchers in general, because pitchers are highly specialized and are not trained to be strong batters. Therefore, a player's estimated batting average will depend strongly on their assumed group.

In typical applications of hierarchical models, each individual's group assignment is provided by the data or design. For example, the practitioners in the therapeutic-touch data were all established and experienced at the same hospital, and therefore it is reasonable to treat their abilities as mutually informative. As another example, in professional baseball most players have an established primary  position (though fielders sometimes change position). And in experimental designs, the group is determined by the manipulated condition of the experiment. However, in situations where the grouping is not clear, it might be possible to extract clusters of similar individuals from the data. Clustering models can be nuanced and challenging, and we will not explore them in this book; for a review see @Wade2023ClusterAnalysis.



<!-- **Memo to me: Also consider relating shrinkage to frequentist estimation** a la Stein's paradox; see <https://www.scientificamerican.com/article/steins-paradox-in-statistics/> and <https://solomonkurz.netlify.app/blog/2019-02-23-stein-s-paradox-and-what-partial-pooling-can-do-for-you/> -->
<!-- Decision: I will not mention Stein's paradox because it is a frequentist concept, and would demand an explanation of sampling distributions and expected values as estimators, etc. Too far afield. -->


<!-- ## Expanding the hierarchy to multiple mints -->

<!-- **Fun as the baseball example is, I'm seriously condsidering omitting it, because it's mostly more of the same...** It doesn't really add a lot conceptually. Mostly it would be useful for *introducing the binomial likelihood*, beyond the bernoulli likelihood. But that could be done later in the chapter on logistic regression... -->

## Exercises

```{r}
#| echo: FALSE
# Initialize global variables for exercise number and part:
exNum <- 0
exPrt <- 0
exNew = function( ){
  exNum <<- exNum+1 # increment global value
  exPrt <<- 0 # reset global value
  return(exNum)
}
exPart = function( ){
  exPrt <<- exPrt+1 # increment global value
  return(LETTERS[exPrt])
}
```


<!-- ------------------------------------------------------------------ -->
\vspace{1ex}\noindent
**Exercise [-@sec-Hierarchical].`r exNew()`.**
*Purpose: Reproduce the therapeutic-touch analysis.*  

Reproduce the analysis of therapeutic-touch data from @sec-therap_touch_in_stan. The data are in package dbda3. 

Extract the MCMC draws and compute diagnostics. Display a *complete* table of the MCMC diagnostics for the parameters. How many parameters are there? (Answer: 30.)


<!-- ------------------------------------------------------------------ -->
\vspace{1ex}\noindent
**Exercise [-@sec-Hierarchical].`r exNew()`.**
*Purpose: See the influence of the prior on shrinkage, as in @fig-shrinkage_kappa.*  

Re-run the therapeutic touch example with two different priors on $\kappa$. 
The only thing to change in each run is the specification of the gamma shape and rate:
```{r}
#| eval: false
gamma_sr <- gamma_s_r_from_mode_sd(mode = 1, sd = 20)  # Change this
gamma_s <- gamma_sr$shape
gamma_r <- gamma_sr$rate
```
\noindent Be sure to assemble that into the `data_list` before re-running the sampling of Stan. There is no need to re-compile the model.

**Part `r exPart()`.**
In one run, set gamma `mode = 0.5` and `sd = 0.5`.
Be sure to generate the *prior* distribution in addition to the posterior.

**Part `r exPart()`.**
In another run, set gamma `mode = 10` and `sd = 100`.
Be sure to generate the *prior* distribution in addition to the posterior.

**Part `r exPart()`.**
Discuss the effect of the prior on the shrinkage of $\theta_j$. Are your results for $\theta_1$ and $\theta_{28}$ similar to what is displayed in @fig-shrinkage_kappa? (They should be.)


<!-- ------------------------------------------------------------------ -->
\vspace{1ex}\noindent
**Exercise [-@sec-Hierarchical].`r exNew()`.**
*Purpose: Write up a concise but thorough report of the therapeutic-touch analysis, respecting the BARG [@Kruschke2021BARG] in @sec-barg.*

This is best done in Quarto or R Markdown, to make the code fully reproducible. If you write reports of your analyses, learning about Quarto or R Markdown will prove to be extremely useful.


<!-- ------------------------------------------------------------------ -->
\vspace{1ex}\noindent
**Exercise [-@sec-Hierarchical].`r exNew()`.**
*Purpose: Explore how amount of data affects shrinkage.*  

@sec-shrinkage_data presented three data sets and discussed how their estimates would be affected by shrinkage. Run each of those data sets through the hierarchical model used for the therapeutic-touch data. There is no need to recompile the model.

*Hint:* The three data sets are included in the dbda3 package as `set_a`, `set_b`, and `set_c`. For example,
```{r}
dbda3::set_a |> str()
```
\noindent Assemble the data into the `data_list` that is input to the sampling function.



<!-- ------------------------------------------------------------------ -->
<!-- \vspace{1ex}\noindent -->
<!-- **Exercise [-@sec-Hierarchical].`r exNew()`.** -->
<!-- *Purpose: ...*   -->


<!-- **Part `r exPart()`.** -->
<!-- Text text text... -->
